Name: Emma Akmakdjian
Title: Marine Debris Grant Coordinator
Research_Interests: Design, Materiality, Marine debris, Ecology
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Art+science, Re-purposed materials, Material science, Interactive/installation design
Sustainability_Contributions: A self-proclaimed ‘creative ecologist,’ Emma likes to challenge dichotomies of art and science in her practice as a mixed media artist by using scientific byproducts including climate data, marine debris, and storytelling to speculate origins of bio cultures and our interconnectedness with the ocean. Fusing her art practice with her scientific work, Emma has been a recurring volunteer on the Marine Debris Grant Project and the Cloud Forest Restoration Project on Santa Rosa Island for the past eight years. Each trip to the island recharges her inspiration to communicate oceanic climate change through art as a catalyst for conversations that confront the way we think about sustainability. Emma is most excited about getting students engaged with the Marine Debris project and expanding a diverse network of ocean activists.
Research_Interests_List: Design, Materiality, Marine debris, Ecology

Name: Athanasios "Alex" Alexandrou
Title: Professor
Research_Interests: Electrification of agricultural equipment, Air quality for agricultural operations, Truck emission control systems, Cold plasma technology and its applications in agriculture, And automation.
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Industrial control systems, Automation.
Sustainability_Contributions: Air quality mitigation and the monitoring of pollutants within agricultural production practices. Work in air quality involves extensive research into the monitoring and suppression of fugitive dust, particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), and organic compounds such as fungal spores and pollen during agricultural field operations. Current work involves the Heavy-Duty Inspection and Maintenance Referee Program (Clean Truck Check) and analyzing agricultural equipment activity data to better understand and manage sector-wide emissions. Served as a consultant on agricultural electrification needs in California and spearheaded research into the environmental benefits of biomass production for the European Union in 2011.
Research_Interests_List: Electrification of agricultural equipment, Air quality for agricultural operations, Truck emission control systems, Cold plasma technology and its applications in agriculture, And automation.

Name: Sean Anderson
Title: Professor
Research_Interests: Centered around three major areas 1) restoring degraded ecosystems, 2) improving the management of the coastal zone, And 3) the scholarship of pedagogy.
Sustainability_Contributions: His coastal zone management work includes quantifying the effects of roads on mobile animals in southern California, evaluating the sustainability of seafood options available for purchase in California, and assessing the impact of pollutants on marine and estuarine systems. He created the NCEAS National Working Group on the Ecotoxicology of Gulf Oil Spill to investigate the long-term ecological consequences of the largest marine oil spill in U.S. history. Most recently he is spearheading a new consortium for the use of Remotely Piloted Systems (UAVs & ROVs) to improve the management of coastal systems. Climate First: Replacing Oil & Gas (CFROG) has awarded $40,000 to Professor Sean Anderson's project: “My People, My Air: Addressing Climate Change through Community Impact.” Professor Anderson and his research team will collect and synthesize air quality data across Ventura County to benefit environmental justice communities across the county.
Research_Interests_List: Centered around three major areas 1) restoring degraded ecosystems, 2) improving the management of the coastal zone, And 3) the scholarship of pedagogy.

Name: Samar Bannister
Title: Director of Marine Programs and Captain of Training Ship Golden Bear
Research_Interests: Maritime
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Maritime
Sustainability_Contributions: Samar Bannister is the Director of Marine Programs and is also the captain for the Training Vessel Golden Bear. This department oversees all shipboard and waterfront training, as well as the professional development of cadets. This comprehensive training program includes rowboats, workboats, tugboats, and the Training Ship Golden Bear (TSGB). The TSGB, owned by the Maritime Administration (MARAD) under the U.S. Department of Transportation and operated by Cal Poly Maritime, serves as the primary training platform for cadets. It provides hands-on application of technological skills introduced in the classroom, leadership development through work assignments, and practical experience with the Cadet Leadership Department. Cadets typically complete two summer sea terms aboard TSGB and one summer training cruise on a commercial vessel. This structured training program fulfills the sea time requirements set by the U.S. Coast Guard and the training competencies mandated by the Standards of Training, Certification, and Watchkeeping (STCW), as established by the International Maritime Organization (IMO). Upon graduation, cadets in the licensed degree programs earn either a U.S. Coast Guard Unlimited Tonnage Third Officer’s License or an Unlimited Horsepower Third Engineer’s License.
Research_Interests_List: Maritime

Name: Brittney Beck
Title:
Faculty/Director of The Citizen Scientist Project
Assistant Professor of Teacher Education
Research_Interests: My research sits at the intersections of climate justice, Ocean literacy, Teacher education, Youth and educator activism, And citizen and community science. as a national geographic explorer and associate professor of teacher education, I am interested in how storytelling, Data, And lived experience come together to shape how young people and educators understand and respond to environmental change. my work explores how classrooms can become spaces where students investigate local environmental issues, Contribute to real scientific and community knowledge, And tell stories with data about the places they care about. through partnerships with schools, Scientists, And communities, I study how inquiry-driven, Place-based learning experiences can empower youth and educators to become informed storytellers, Investigators, And advocates working toward more just and sustainable environmental futures.
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: My teaching interests focus on the integration of science, Social studies, And environmental education within teacher education programs, With an emphasis on community-embedded and place-based teaching. i am particularly interested in preparing future educators to help students investigate environmental issues through interdisciplinary approaches that connect ecological systems, Civic decision making, Cultural knowledge, And community perspectives. my teaching emphasizes inquiry, Storytelling with data, And citizen and community science as pathways for engaging youth in understanding and responding to environmental challenges in the places where they live.
Sustainability_Contributions: My sustainability contributions include securing and leading grant-supported initiatives, including projects funded by the United States Department of Education and the National Geographic Society, that build capacity for citizen and community science in schools and teacher education programs. Through these efforts, I work to equip educators and students with tools to investigate issues such as air quality, biodiversity, and water systems while developing pathways for youth civic engagement. I also collaborate with California State Parks to convene scientists, storytellers, and educators in shared learning experiences that draw inspiration from the land and from one another, helping translate place-based environmental knowledge into meaningful educational practice. In addition, I contribute to National Geographic’s Pristine Seas initiative, where I help develop ocean literacy and conservation education programs in collaboration with youth, educators, universities, and ministries of education around the world, supporting the integration of science, storytelling, and education in efforts to protect marine ecosystems.
Research_Interests_List: My research sits at the intersections of climate justice, Ocean literacy, Teacher education, Youth and educator activism, And citizen and community science. as a national geographic explorer and associate professor of teacher education, I am interested in how storytelling, Data, And lived experience come together to shape how young people and educators understand and respond to environmental change. my work explores how classrooms can become spaces where students investigate local environmental issues, Contribute to real scientific and community knowledge, And tell stories with data about the places they care about. through partnerships with schools, Scientists, And communities, I study how inquiry-driven, Place-based learning experiences can empower youth and educators to become informed storytellers, Investigators, And advocates working toward more just and sustainable environmental futures.

Name: Melissa Beresford
Title: Associate Professor
Research_Interests: Water insecurity, Resource distribution, Social norms, Institutional economics, Field methods, Qualitative data analysis, Cross-cultural research
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Beresford’s primary teaching foci include research methods training, Experiential learning (undergraduate research experiences), And ecological, Economic, And applied anthropology.
Sustainability_Contributions: Dr. Beresford investigates the norms and social infrastructures that enable people adapt to water and other resources insecurities, especially in the context of the global water crisis and accelerating climate change. Funded by an NSF CAREER grant, she is currently leading an international group of scholars researching how “moral economies” (a specific type of norm-based social system) allow people to cope with and adapt to water insecurity cross-culturally. Additionally, she collaborates within the Water Insecurity Community of Practice (WISE-CP), working to develop new experiential metrics for household water insecurity in United States and other high-income contexts. As an ethnographer and methodologist who specializes in field methods and qualitative data analysis, she grounds her scholarship in community-based mixed-methods field work with water insecure households in California and the broader U.S. West.
Research_Interests_List: Water insecurity, Resource distribution, Social norms, Institutional economics, Field methods, Qualitative data analysis, Cross-cultural research

Sustainability_Contributions: Professor Brown Blake's recent projects include Climate justice in nursing education. She updated her courses to encourage students to examine how nursing and climate change intersect and impact their lives. At the faculty level, Brown Blake has encouraged her colleagues to incorporate more solutions and case studies on climate change and less emphasis on problem-focused lectures. This change resulted in Chico State joining the national Nurses Climate Challenge, an initiative that challenges nursing programs to demonstrate their commitment to teaching innovative content that addresses climate change and its impact on health.

Name: Karen Flagg
Title: Cloud Forest Restoration Technician
Sustainability_Contributions: Karen has been working on the Channel Islands for over twenty years collaborating and partnering with agencies, nonprofits and others who love the islands. Karen’s first island project was to grow native plants for seabird habitat on Scorpion Rock. Karen continued to work on the islands building a nursery on Santa Barbara Island, growing plants to support the nesting requirements of Cassin’s Aucklets and Scripps’s murrelets. On Santa Cruz Island, under the direction of USGS Kathryn McEachern, Karen worked towards the recovery of rare and endangered plant species, propagating Malacothamnus fasciculatus var. nesioticus, Island bush mallow and monitoring other endangered species. In 2016 Karen accompanied Kathryn to Santa Rosa Island to evaluate the nursery and develop a plan for its future use in propagating plants for the Cloud Forest Project. Over the next few years Karen brought SBCC students to the island to “learn by doing” on island projects.

Name: Joseph Kim
Title: Chair, Civil Engineering & Construction Engineering Management
Research_Interests: As a director of green bim laboratory, Dr. kim focuses on sustainable design and construction in built environment and infrastructure, Advanced construction scheduling using optimization techniques, Building information modeling, Cost estimating methods, International construction, Information technology in construction operations, Project delivery systems, And statistical methods for construction engineers
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Sustainability in the built environment, Sustainability in infrastructure systems, Sustainability and green construction
Sustainability_Contributions: Internationally recognized for his research in sustainability for building and infrastructure systems, Dr. Kim has published 34 journal articles, contributed to 47 peer-reviewed conference proceedings, and delivered 36 oral and poster presentations at national and international conferences. Of these, 10 journal articles, 13 proceedings, and 48 presentations are directly related to sustainability and environmental engineering. A few keynote speeches include the following: - “Current Research Status for Recycled Construction Materials for Better Built Environments” at the 2024 Sustainable Materials, Environment and Technologies under Climate Change Scenario. - “Recycled Construction Materials: Current Status and Future Research Directions” at the 2024 13th International Conference on Environment, Energy and Biotechnology. Dr. Kim’s dedication to undergraduate research has also been nationally recognized. His collaborative work with undergraduate students earned Best Research Presentation at the 2014 SACNAS National Conference for “Green Building Materials: Fabricating Sandwich Panels for Structural Use”, and was featured at the 30th Anniversary National Conference on Undergraduate Research for “Fabricating and Testing Composite Sandwich Panel Specimens Made of Green Building Materials.”.
Research_Interests_List: As a director of green bim laboratory, Dr. kim focuses on sustainable design and construction in built environment and infrastructure, Advanced construction scheduling using optimization techniques, Building information modeling, Cost estimating methods, International construction, Information technology in construction operations, Project delivery systems, And statistical methods for construction engineers

Name: Alison O'Dowd
Title: Professor

Plays a significant role in student sustainability training, mentoring undergraduates and graduate students in applied field research.

Name: Scott Shaffer
Title: Professor of Biological Sciences
Research_Interests: Professor shaffer's research lab primarily focuses on seabirds as model to understand how animals exploit marine resources across vast ocean scapes and coastal habitats. this includes the use of biologging tools (e.g. tracking devices, Accelerometers, Time depth recorders) to explore the behavioral flexibility, Effort, And distribution of seabirds that forage throughout the north pacific.
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: He teaches courses related to ecology and evolution, And marine biology including conservation biology, Ecology, Experimental sampling design and analysis, And physiological ecology
Sustainability_Contributions: His research adds to the ecological base of knowledge about marine animals and their habitat use, which supports the creation of or maintenance of marine protected areas. In a more applied capacity, he is a part of a team focused on understanding seabird interactions with vessels at sea to reduce bycatch. Finally, he is a member of several working groups focused on the potential impacts of ocean wind energy development on marine megafauna.

Name: Monica C. So
Title: Associate Professor
Research_Interests: The long-term research goals of the so lab are three-fold: (i) to study structure-property relationships of self-assembled materials, (ii) to characterize self-assembled materials on surfaces, And (iii) to study their optical, Photophysical, Electrochemical, And optoelectronic behaviors.
Sustainability_Contributions: The So Lab harnesses chemical intuition to approach fundamental challenges in physics and tackle sustainability problems. Their research has important environmental implications, such as improving the long-term stability of solar panels on our rooftop, improving the performance of Li-S batteries, and as well as more effectively cleaning up water after wildfires and oil spills. She is also the principal investigator for The BATTERIES Team which researches lithium sulfur batteries to create a greener world.
Research_Interests_List: The long-term research goals of the so lab are three-fold: (i) to study structure-property relationships of self-assembled materials, (ii) to characterize self-assembled materials on surfaces, And (iii) to study their optical, Photophysical, Electrochemical, And optoelectronic behaviors.

Name: Anne Marie Todd

Research_Interests: Sense of place, Environmental activism, Climate pedagogy
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Environmental communication, Rhetoric, And popular culture
Sustainability_Contributions: Dean Todd Developed an interdisciplinary course on Global Climate Change. Her work examines the way environmental rhetoric shapes our community history and sense of place. Her most recent book, Valley of Heart's Delight: Environment and Sense of Place in the Santa Clara Valley, focuses on the agricultural history of Silicon Valley. Her book, Communicating Environmental Patriotism: A Rhetorical History of the American Environmental Movement received the Christine Oravec Environmental Communication Research Award. She has also published more than 30 scholarly journal articles and book chapters, including research on climate literacy, gardening and food activism, and environmental messages in popular culture. Dean Todd is a founding member of the International Environmental Communication Association and served on the advisory board of the Green Ninja Project. Former chair of the University Sustainability Board.

Name: Tammie Visintainer
College: Connie L. Lurie College of Education & College of Science (joint appointment)
Title: Assistant Professor of Teacher Education
Research_Interests: Secondary science education, Climate justice, Climate science education
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Secondary science methods (single subject credential program), Climate justice action research (graduate course)
Sustainability_Contributions: Professor Visintainer is a recipient of an NSF Faculty Early Career Development Program (CAREER) award entitled: "CAREER - Transforming Science Teaching and Learning through Empowering Teachers and Students as Climate Justice Action Researchers and Change Agents." Her research focuses on climate justice and community health. Climate justice action research (CJAR) combines elements of community-based science and youth participatory action research to support teachers and students as action researchers and change agents in their school communities focusing on the climate justice issue of urban and extreme heat. Teachers participate in a year-long professional learning experience where they gain the content knowledge and research practices needed to design and implement CJAR curricular units during the school year. Students identify local issues of heat, collect science (e.g., temperature), mapping (e.g., percent tree canopy cover), and community experience data (e.g., oral histories with elders), create arts-based science stories to share their findings with the public, and advocate for change.
Research_Interests_List: Secondary science education, Climate justice, Climate science education

Name: Richard Wallace
College: College of the Arts, Humanities & Social Sciences
Title: Professor and Director
Research_Interests: Cultural anthropology, Economic anthropology, Conservation and development, Latin american studies, Brazilian and colombian amazon.
Sustainability_Contributions: Professor Wallace is interested in how cultural anthropology can inform our understanding of conservation and development challenges in the Amazon, and contribute to the challenge of solving them. He did a research project; Exploring Forest Community-Market Interactions in Acre, Brazil. This project examined the interaction between forest communities and urban market tradesmen (and women) and businesses. It was conducted in collaboration with the Acre Research and Extension in Agroforestry Group (PESACRE), a local non-governmental organization which works with forest communities in the region to promote community development, sustainable agroforestry and conservation. Their research examined how communities and enterprises interact at the business trade fair of the FLORA fair, a forest product trade fair held in Rio Branco, which brings together community leaders and urban entrepreneurs in a relaxed business setting to negotiate contracts for forest-based food and artisan products.
Research_Interests_List: Cultural anthropology, Economic anthropology, Conservation and development, Latin american studies, Brazilian and colombian amazon.

Name: Jenna Wong
Title: Assistant Professor
Research_Interests: Sustainable structural resilience, Performance-based earthquake engineering, Nonstructural behavior, Modular housing
Teaching_Interests: Teaching Interests: Implementation of technology in engineering curriculum (i.e. virtual reality)
Sustainability_Contributions: Investigating how roof gardens, vertical forests, and vegetated walls influence structural performance. This work is supported by an NSF CAREER award and is generating the first data of its kind to quantify how natural hazards and life-cycle effects impact structural dynamic response. Additional research investigates modular "tiny home" housing - an increasingly popular solution in major metropolitan areas facing housing shortages. Recent computational modeling and experimental testing have revealed concerning seismic performance for these structures, underscoring the need for continued investigation. This research aims to integrate the principles of sustainability and structural resilience, fostering new approaches to meet the evolving needs of 21st-century communities.
Notes: Contributor to: She is a member of ASCE, EERI, SEAONC, CAIES, and SWE.
Research_Interests_List: Sustainable structural resilience, Performance-based earthquake engineering, Nonstructural behavior, Modular housing
